name: Black History
description: Landmark figures, movements, and moments in African-American history.

questions.q: Who is widely considered the leader of the American civil rights movement and delivered the 'I Have a Dream' speech?

source.notes: Speech delivered August 28, 1963 at the March on Washington.

questions.q: Rosa Parks's 1955 refusal to give up her seat on a city bus sparked a year-long boycott in which Alabama city?

source.notes: Boycott ran December 1955 - December 1956.

questions.q: Who became the first African-American President of the United States, elected in 2008?

source.notes: Inaugurated January 20, 2009.

questions.q: Which abolitionist made about 13 trips on the Underground Railroad, guiding dozens of enslaved people to freedom?

source.notes: Later appears on the proposed redesigned $20 bill.

questions.q: The Emancipation Proclamation, issued by which president in 1863, declared enslaved people in Confederate states free?

source.notes: Effective January 1, 1863.

questions.q: Juneteenth — now a federal holiday — commemorates what 1865 event?
choices: News of emancipation reaching Galveston, Texas

source.notes: June 19, 1865, when Union Gen. Granger arrived at Galveston.

questions.q: Which poet penned the 1978 autobiography 'I Know Why the Caged Bird Sings' and the poem 'Still I Rise'?

source.notes: Angelou also spoke at Clinton's 1993 inauguration.

questions.q: Which landmark 1954 Supreme Court case ruled that racially segregated public schools were unconstitutional?

source.notes: Argued by Thurgood Marshall for the NAACP.

questions.q: Which African-American jurist argued Brown v. Board and later became the first Black Supreme Court justice?

source.notes: Marshall served 1967-1991.

questions.q: Malcolm X was assassinated in 1965 while delivering a speech at which New York venue?

source.notes: Manhattan, February 21, 1965.

questions.q: The Voting Rights Act of 1965 was signed by which U.S. president?

source.notes: Signed August 6, 1965.

questions.q: Which 1960s radical organization, founded by Huey P. Newton and Bobby Seale, called for armed community patrols?

source.notes: Founded Oakland, 1966.

questions.q: Which Harlem Renaissance author wrote the 1937 novel 'Their Eyes Were Watching God'?

source.notes: Alice Walker later revived Hurston's reputation.

questions.q: Who was the first African-American woman elected to the U.S. Congress, in 1968?

source.notes: Chisholm also ran for president in 1972.

questions.q: In 1947, who broke Major League Baseball's color barrier as the first Black player in the modern era?

source.notes: Signed by the Brooklyn Dodgers' Branch Rickey.

questions.q: Which holiday, created by Dr. Maulana Karenga in 1966, honors African heritage over seven days starting December 26?

source.notes: Celebrated Dec 26 - Jan 1.

questions.q: Which African-American woman became the first Black mayor of a major U.S. city when Cleveland elected her in 1967?

source.notes: Actually a man — Carl Stokes, Cleveland, 1967.

questions.q: Which 1963 event saw Martin Luther King Jr. jailed briefly and produce his famous 'Letter from Birmingham Jail'?

source.notes: King was arrested on April 12, 1963.

questions.q: Hiram Revels became the first Black U.S. Senator in 1870, representing which state?

source.notes: Seated February 25, 1870.

questions.q: Which historian, known as the 'Father of Black History,' launched Negro History Week in 1926?

source.notes: Negro History Week grew into Black History Month (1976).

questions.q: In 1989, which Army general became the first African-American to chair the Joint Chiefs of Staff?

source.notes: Powell later served as Secretary of State.

questions.q: The 1919 'Red Summer' was a period of racial violence across the U.S. A particularly brutal week of that summer's violence hit which Illinois city?

source.notes: The Chicago Race Riot ran July 27 - August 3, 1919.
